item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Posting here for clarity.

Patricia Piccinini does indeed own the sculpture, as well as this image. As she has not indicated releasing this image under Creative Commons 3.0, this image is assumed not compliant and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could keep going, but there's no use battering a dead horse. I hope you can fix it so it becomes upvote worthy!

Unlikely, since they haven't been active on the wiki for three years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know exactly why it's so low rated: The english is just… bad. Really, really poor. I'm not talking about the tone here (altought it's also terrible), mind you, just about the language itself; sentences don't make sense, most of the text feels disconnected and random. Expresions and entire paragraphs are worded so simply that make you want to laugh. I don't want to sound mean, but while I read it I had the feeling that the whole scip had been written by a little boy that hadn't really mastered his english and that really detracts from the whole thing. The idea itself may be good. But the way it's written certainly makes you want to downvote hard.

Also: the interview feels kind of: "In your face!" it leaves nothing to the imagination. There's no mistery here, nothing to grab you. This Lamia may as well be a cardboard cutout with a post-it detailing its entire history and motivation. It's not a complex being at all, it's almost goofy.

I could keep going, but there's no use battering a dead horse. I hope you can fix it so it becomes upvote worthy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: don't know why its so low rated, +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's OK. The concept is quite scary, and the picture is nasty, but I feel the effects are a bit too vaguely explained, and the origin far too clearly explained. I think it would work much better with clearly-defined effects, and mere hints at a backstory.

Also, the interview log seems a little clunkily written.

No vote for now, but I'll check back, and hope for a better version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus Christ

I don't think I've seen a more horrifying set of artworks. Every damned one of those could be an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing is sort of messed up. The interview has a lot of wording problems, and you are incredibly vague…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is somewhat messily written: the creature's designation keeps jumping from 1458 to 1458-1; the perception-altering effect is described so weirdly that I have very little idea who sees the creature as what and which form is the true one; the effect also doesn't add anything to the article; the creature is implied to be able to walk but I can't see any way it would be able to. The interview is rather meh as well.

I also feel that the primary effect isn't sufficiently explored, and the overall story isn't that interesting. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the greek myth twist. The development is pretty interesting, and overall i like the character. The dialog, though, is a little forced. I'm not sure how to help it, but it is difficult.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to like this, but I was meh until I got to the interview, and then the stilted dialog pushed me into downvote territory. Shame, as it's quite a nice concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ill do my best to fix that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lamia didnt become the basis for the article till I was over halfway through writing it, After Athena_Grey suggested the similarities and that I could link it to Lamia for a decent hook, If that's what youre implying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting that Lamia is used as the basis for a article so shortly after Scylla was used in 1823. Scylla was the only child of Lamia to be left alive albeit in a cursed form.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll start out by saying that I really like the concept for this SCP so that the rest of this post doesn't immediately sound like a mindless hate train, but there are a lot of things wrong with it at present.

There's sentences which don't make sense, be it from missing words or confusing structure.
The SCP's sapience isn't referred to in the description; only its ability for telephatic communication. I get that it's implied and obvious, but an SCP's sapience is always something the Foundation would note. Maybe it should be tied it with the mentioning of its ability to communicate.
SCP-1485-2 is mentioned in the primary picture but never again. Is it supposed to be SCP-1485-1?
How do we know about SCP-1485's effects if only one child has been observed under its effects? The only way we'd know that it had the ability to imprint itself on children as it does is with testing involving more kids, the repercussions and results of which don't seem to be present.
The paragraph involving the SCP's capture is very poorly written… and how does a hu-manatee (see what I did there?) manage to get into a shopping mall?

There's more, but you get the gist. Good concept, but with critical flaws.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Found it kind of boring, and the myth as hook doesn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 10 whole nickels for you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By the way, the image is of Australian sculptor Patricia Piccinini's 2008 sculpture 'The Long Awaited'. A gallery of images and commentary on the sculpture, and other works, can be found on the artist's (admittedly hard to navigate) site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's Lamia, isn't it!

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lamia

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. This annoyed me through the whole article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You established that SCP-1458 is the critter. There's no point in referring to it as SCP-1458-1. Make -1 the kid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure how i feel about this one yet, so no vote either way. I think it certainly shows potential though. Maybe i just need to read it again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She used to be human. Then she got made into a wierd manatee creature. Then she killed lots of children and now she's old and she feels like crap about it and she just wants her children back. Id say thats a curse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ..that doesn't sound like a curse, that sounds like "standard human reaction to the murder of their spawn".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed, Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the scp but there's a point at the interview that says:

Note: At this point in the interview SCP-XXXX-1 began vocalizing a low pitched rumbling noise

Shouldn't it be SCP-1458-1? :3

Despite of that, as I said I like this SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 10 nickels to whoever can

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the usage of Greek myth here, though I can't remember who it was that it was forever haunted by her children's deaths. That picture is also nice, so I guess I'll give it an upvote.

item.title: Credits
item.content:encoded:
A big thank you to everyone in chat who helped me with it and put up with my constant asking!

Special mentions to Annalea, Voct and Athena_Grey.
